learn.title: Solar as Architecture: BIPV
learn.intro: Buildings consume huge energy for cooling and lighting. Building Integrated Photovoltaics turn roofs, facades and windows into power generators, cutting grid demand while serving as construction material. This dual function accelerates clean energy adoption in cities where land is scarce and emissions from buildings are rising fast.
learn.c1_text: BIPV replaces conventional building parts with solar-active materials like glass or shingles. Think of a rooftop tile that both sheds rain and makes 100 W per square meter. Unlike bolt-on panels, BIPV is the skin of the building, saving material and space in dense urban areas.
learn.c2_text: BIPV includes curtain walls, skylights and balcony rails. A south-facing facade in sunny climate can yield 80-120 kWh/m2 yearly. Modules must meet building codes for insulation and safety, so laminated glass with cells inside is common, blending energy generation with weather protection seamlessly.
learn.c3_text: India's PM Surya Ghar scheme promotes rooftop solar on homes. BIPV fits new smart cities like GIFT City, Gujarat, where facade panels cut peak load. With 300 sunny days, BIPV on offices can offset 20-30% of annual electricity, supporting the 500 GW RE target by 2030.
learn.stat2: BIPV can cover 30% of building energy need
learn.stat3: US DOE supports BIPV cost reduction programs
learn.stat4: Solar buildings can save 50% HVAC energy

learn.title: Perovskite Solar Cells
learn.intro: Silicon solar panels dominate but plateau near 22% efficiency. Perovskite cells offer cheaper, flexible, higher-efficiency alternatives using abundant materials. They could slash solar costs and enable transparent windows. NREL research pushes tandem cells past 33%, vital for land-limited clean energy expansion worldwide.
learn.c1_text: Perovskites are crystal structures like CH3NH3PbI3 that absorb light efficiently. Imagine a paint that turns sunlight to power at 25% rate versus silicon's 22%. They are made by low-cost coating, like printing, enabling thin films on curved surfaces unlike brittle silicon wafers.
learn.c2_text: Stacking perovskite on silicon forms tandem cells capturing more spectrum. NREL achieved 33.9% lab efficiency, near theoretical 34% limit. This beats single silicon by 10 points, meaning a 1 kW rooftop gives more daily energy, reducing panel area needed by about 30% for same output.
learn.c3_text: India's National Solar Mission explores perovskite via IIT Bombay labs. With 290 GW solar goal by 2030, local manufacturing of perovskite films could cut import of silicon. Pilot lines in Hyderabad target 1 GW yearly capacity, lowering LCOE below ₹2 per kWh by 2027.

learn.title: Wind Turbine End-of-Life
learn.intro: Wind turbines last 20-25 years. Thousands of blades will retire soon, posing waste challenges as they are fiberglass. IRENA guides recycling to recover materials and cut landfill. Proper end-of-life management keeps wind truly green and supports circular economy in growing RE sectors.
learn.c1_text: Turbines run 20-25 years with gearbox oil changes every 6 months. A 2 MW turbine blade is 50 m long; imagine a football field rotating. Maintenance via drones cuts cost 20%. After 25 years, 90% of steel tower is recycled but blades need special processes.
learn.c2_text: Blades are glass fiber and epoxy, hard to separate. IRENA notes co-processing in cement kilns recovers 60% material, replacing coal. New pyrolysis melts resin to reuse fibers. By 2030, 2.2 Mt blades will retire globally; recycling avoids 1 Mt landfill waste annually.
learn.c3_text: India has 42 GW wind; Tamil Nadu hubs face old blade waste. Suzlon recycles at Coimbatore plant, recovering 70% fiber. Policy under Swachh Bharat pushes blade reuse in cement. By 2025, 5,000 tonnes blades retire yearly, prompting local recycling parks near Muppandal wind farm.

learn.title: Solid-State Batteries
learn.intro: Lithium-ion batteries use flammable liquid electrolytes. Solid-state batteries replace them with solid ceramics, boosting safety and energy density. DOE says they can double EV range and charge faster, key to clean transport and grid storage without fire risks limiting adoption.
learn.c1_text: Solid-state uses ceramic or sulfide instead of liquid. Think of a sponge replaced by solid rock that still moves ions. It removes fire risk; a nail test shows no flame versus li-ion explosion. Energy density reaches 500 Wh/kg, about double current 250 Wh/kg cells.
learn.c2_text: Higher density means lighter EV packs. DOE notes 80% charge in 15 min possible due to stable interface. Solid lithium metal anode holds more ions. A 70 kWh pack shrinks 30%, adding 200 km range. No thermal runaway simplifies cooling, cutting system weight further.
learn.c3_text: India's battery mission funds solid-state via ISRO and R&D at ARCI. With 30% EV sales target by 2030, local cells avoid imports. A Hyderabad pilot aims 100 MWh yearly; FAME-II subsidy supports safe buses using prototypes achieving 450 Wh/kg by 2026.

learn.title: Hydrogen Fuel Cells
learn.intro: Hydrogen fuel cells make electricity from H2 and air, emitting only water. They power zero-emission vehicles with fast refueling. DOE highlights their role where batteries fall short, like trucks and trains, crucial for decarbonizing transport and reaching net-zero targets efficiently.
learn.c1_text: A fuel cell stacks hydrogen and oxygen with catalyst. Imagine a battery fed by gas; H2 splits to electrons making current, water as byproduct. A 100 kW unit runs a bus, giving 500 km range. Efficiency 60%, double diesel engine, with silent operation and no tailpipe pollution.
learn.c2_text: Fuel cell electric vehicles refill in 5 min, like petrol. DOE notes heavy trucks benefit as batteries get too heavy over 500 km. Toyota Mirai uses 5 kg H2 for 650 km. Stack life 8,000 hours; recycling platinum catalyst recovers 95% of precious metal for reuse.
learn.c3_text: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission targets 5 MMT by 2030. NTPC runs fuel cell buses in Leh using Himalayan hydro H2. Reliance builds electrolyzers in Jamnagar. With ₹19,744 crore outlay, trucks on Delhi-Mumbai expressway may use cells cutting 1 Mt CO2 yearly by 2027.

learn.title: Wind Resource Assessment
learn.intro: Good wind sites make or break projects. IRENA outlines assessment using meteorology and GIS to find areas with steady 7 m/s winds. Accurate mapping cuts risk and cost, enabling India and others to deploy turbines where clean power is maximal and land use is optimized.
learn.c1_text: Developers use mast anemometers at 100 m height for 1 year. Imagine a giant ruler catching breeze speed; 7 m/s average suits turbines. LIDAR scans save cost 40% versus mast. Data on turbulence guides model; a 50 MW site needs 3 masts to confirm resource before build.
learn.c2_text: IRENA recommends layering wind maps with land use to avoid farms. Software predicts output within 10% error. Exclusions like forests cut area 30%. A coastal zone with 8 m/s yields 35% more than inland 6 m/s, so mapping boosts capacity factor from 25% to 40% realistically.
learn.c3_text: India's NIWE provides wind atlas at 100 m. Gujarat and Tamil Nadu scored 7.5 m/s, attracting 15 GW. The 2022 IRENA method aided Kutch survey finding 20 GW potential. C-WET masts number 900 nationwide, helping states meet 140 GW wind target by 2030 with optimized sites.
